stories.headline: PAGASA issues flood advisories across six regions as low-pressure area approaches Philippine coast
stories.summary: PAGASA issued general flood advisories on June 29 for Mimaropa, Bicol, Western, Central and Eastern Visayas, and Caraga as a low-pressure area about 390 kilometers east of Guiuan, Eastern Samar was assessed to have a high chance of developing into a tropical cyclone within 24 hours. The system was expected to traverse Southern Luzon and the Visayas on June 30 before moving over the West Philippine Sea around July 1, when further intensification was forecast. Authorities in Bicol imposed travel restrictions on small sea vessels and ordered preemptive evacuations in hazard-prone areas.
stories.watch_for: PAGASA said the system would be named Henry if it develops into a tropical cyclone within Philippine territory and forecast it to emerge over the West Philippine Sea around July 1.

stories.headline: Senate impeachment court sets 92-day trial schedule and approves 17 shared witnesses for Duterte proceedings
stories.summary: The Senate impeachment court approved a 92-trial-day schedule for Vice President Sara Duterte's impeachment proceedings in a 15-page pre-trial order signed by Senate President Sherwin Gatchalian, with 62 days allocated to the prosecution and 30 to the defense. The prosecution named 57 witnesses and the defense 45, with 17 shared witnesses including former senator Antonio Trillanes IV and Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla. The trial is scheduled to begin on July 6.
stories.watch_for: The trial is set to open July 6 at 2 p.m. and run on Mondays, Tuesdays, and Wednesdays through July 27, then shift to Tuesdays, Wednesdays, and Thursdays at 3 p.m. thereafter.

stories.headline: Defense team in Duterte impeachment agrees to allow BIR tax records box to be marked as evidence
stories.summary: Vice President Sara Duterte's defense lawyers indicated they are not opposed to the prosecution marking a sealed green cardboard box containing Duterte's BIR tax records as evidence in her Senate impeachment trial, House Prosecutor Leila de Lima said. The defense requested that the box be opened in executive rather than open session. The box, received during House committee hearings in April, remained sealed with BIR Commissioner Charlito Mendoza's signature affixed; prosecutors say it is essential to substantiate allegations of unexplained wealth against the Vice President.

stories.headline: Ombudsman rejects DOJ findings in Villanueva flood funds case, to conduct own probe
stories.summary: Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla said his office rejected the findings of the Department of Justice in a case involving Senator Joel Villanueva's alleged role in flood control project anomalies and will conduct its own preliminary investigation before deciding whether to file charges at the Sandiganbayan. A former DPWH engineer alleged Villanueva arranged a P600 million allocation to Bulacan's first district in exchange for a cut, while another alleged he requested P1.5 billion for a multi-purpose building. Villanueva denied the allegations, which remain unproven.
stories.context: A related preliminary investigation into Senator Francis Escudero's alleged involvement in similar anomalies is already underway; both senators have denied any role.

stories.headline: Senate president renews push to ban social media for minors after Tacloban school shooting
stories.summary: Senate President Sherwin Gatchalian called for renewed action on legislation banning children aged 16 and under from social media, citing concerns about violent content as a factor in the June 22 shooting at San Jose National High School in Tacloban that killed three students and injured 20. Gatchalian's Social Media Safety for Children Act, Senate Bill No. 2066, would require platforms to enforce strict age verification and parental controls and redesign features that encourage compulsive use, with violations potentially leading to suspension or revocation of operating authority.
stories.context: The Philippine Pediatric Society recommends against social media for children under 16. A separate debate has emerged over whether to lower the minimum age of criminal responsibility from 15 to 12, with the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Council opposing the change.

stories.headline: Congress approves basic education voucher bill to allow students to enroll in participating private schools
stories.summary: A bicameral conference committee approved the Basic Education Voucher Program Act, which would allow Filipino students to use government vouchers to enroll in participating private schools, aimed at easing overcrowding in public classrooms. The bill reconciles Senate Bill No. 1981 and House Bill No. 4744, amending the Government Assistance to Students and Teachers in Private Education Act. Participating private schools would be required to undergo quality assurance reviews and publish performance data.

stories.headline: House prosecution panel for Duterte impeachment trial expands to 26 members ahead of July 6 start
stories.summary: The House prosecution team pursuing Vice President Sara Duterte's conviction in her Senate impeachment trial grew from 11 to 26 members after four private prosecutors and 11 legislators were added to the panel, Manila Bulletin reported. The four new private counsel are Theodore Te, Reynaldo Robles, Arnold Labay, and Mae Divinagracia, bringing the total of private prosecutors to 15. Former House quad-committee chairman Robert Ace Barbers was also named as a fourth prosecution spokesperson.

stories.headline: Senator Lacson says Bonoan must repay at least P1 billion in public funds alongside state witness cooperation
stories.summary: Senator Panfilo Lacson said former Public Works Secretary Manuel Bonoan should repay at least P1 billion to the government alongside any state witness cooperation agreement, based on testimony from a former DPWH undersecretary about P5 billion in annual projects with an average 15 percent kickback commitment. Lacson said Bonoan's disclosures could expose officials who dealt with the department and warned that those with direct dealings must be quaking in their boots. Bonoan is a co-accused with Senator Jinggoy Estrada in a P573 million flood control plunder case before the Sandiganbayan.
stories.context: The Ombudsman earlier announced that Bonoan signed a cooperation agreement to serve as state witness and that prosecutors would seek his discharge from the plunder case.

stories.headline: Oil firms to raise pump prices on June 30 as Middle East tensions ease following Iran-US talks
stories.summary: Oil firms will implement higher fuel prices effective Tuesday, June 30, with Pilipinas Shell announcing increases of P1.65 per liter for gasoline, P0.84 for diesel, and P1.22 for kerosene, GMA News Online reported. The Department of Energy said global oil markets were calming following developments in the Middle East, including talks between Iran and the United States on halting hostilities around the Strait of Hormuz. DOE Secretary Sharon Garin said prices appeared to be settling toward more stable levels.

stories.headline: Finance secretary promotes investment in 901-megawatt offshore wind project in Camarines Sur
stories.summary: Finance Secretary Frederick Go met with executives from Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners to advance the San Miguel Bay Offshore Wind Power Project in Camarines Sur, described as Southeast Asia's potential first offshore wind farm with a planned capacity of 901 megawatts, GMA News Online reported. The project is a partnership involving Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners and ACEN Corporation. Go said offshore wind development is critical to the country's long-term energy security and sustainability goals and would attract global companies seeking renewable energy sources.

stories.headline: Two electrical poles collapse on Navotas bridge, injuring two people and cutting power to Tondo hospital
stories.summary: Two electrical poles on Marala bridge in North Bay Boulevard at the boundary of Navotas City and Manila collapsed on the evening of June 29, injuring two people and causing a power outage that affected Tondo Medical Center and nearby homes in Navotas and Tondo, GMA News Online reported. One of the injured was taken to Tondo Medical Center and the other to Philippine Orthopedic Center. A Meralco engineer said only one transformer required repair; the bridge was closed to vehicles while power was restored. The cause of the collapse was under investigation.

stories.headline: Three children drown in excavated pit along Laguna de Bay shore while collecting clams
stories.summary: Three minors drowned in an excavated pit along the shore of Laguna de Bay, including a 13-year-old and a 12-year-old who were found embracing each other when their bodies were recovered, and a 6-year-old boy found nearby, GMA News Online reported citing a television broadcast. The families of the victims asked that the excavation be filled to prevent future accidents. The barangay said it was coordinating with those responsible for the excavation to address the hazard.

stories.headline: Fire sweeps through Cagayan de Oro residential area, destroying around 90 homes and displacing 190 families
stories.summary: A fire spread through Barangay Lapasan in Cagayan de Oro City, destroying approximately 90 houses and displacing about 190 families, GMA News Online reported. The blaze spread rapidly because the homes were closely spaced and built from lightweight materials. Authorities estimated property damage at P1.7 million.

stories.headline: 12-year-old student surrenders to police after allegedly stabbing classmate outside Bacolod school
stories.summary: A 12-year-old Grade 7 student surrendered to police in Bacolod City on the afternoon of June 29, about an hour after allegedly stabbing a 14-year-old Grade 8 student outside Sum-ag National High School, the Inquirer reported. The victim suffered minor stab wounds to the left arm and left side and was taken to a nearby hospital. The 12-year-old suspect, classified as a child in conflict with the law, surrendered voluntarily to Police Station 9.

stories.headline: Tab Baldwin attends DOLE hearing; lawyers argue permanent residency exempts him from work permit requirement
stories.summary: Former Ateneo de Manila University basketball coach Tab Baldwin attended a clarificatory hearing at the Department of Labor and Employment on June 29, where his legal team argued that his permanent resident status in the Philippines exempts him from the requirement to obtain an Alien Employment Permit. Labor Secretary Francis Tolentino cited a January 2025 memorandum requiring foreign workers who are otherwise exempt from the AEP to secure a Certificate of Exemption, which Baldwin had not obtained. DOLE gave Baldwin 10 days to submit documents and will summon ADMU on July 9.
stories.context: The DOLE investigation was triggered by a June 8 incident in Aurora in which two Ateneo basketball players drowned during a team activity.
stories.watch_for: Baldwin has 10 days to submit documents to DOLE; the next hearing involving ADMU is scheduled for July 9.

stories.headline: Venezuela earthquake death toll reaches 1,719 as aftershock strikes during active rescue operations
stories.summary: Twin earthquakes of magnitudes 7.2 and 7.5 have killed at least 1,719 people in northwestern Venezuela, with 5,034 injured and nearly 16,000 homeless, according to figures announced by Venezuelan National Assembly leader Jorge Rodriguez. The hardest-hit area is the northern port city of La Guaira, where a 4.6-magnitude aftershock struck on June 29 during ongoing rescue efforts. Interim President Delcy Rodriguez said rescue operations had not been suspended, citing continued reports of survivors being found.
stories.context: Venezuela was already facing deep political and economic crisis before the earthquakes. Approximately 30,000 local emergency workers and 2,700 foreign experts from 24 countries are involved in the rescue effort, with over 500 tonnes of international aid delivered.

stories.headline: US Supreme Court rules presidents can fire heads of independent federal agencies without cause
stories.summary: The US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to give the president broad authority to dismiss heads of independent federal agencies without justification, overturning a 1935 precedent that had protected such officials. The decision affects positions at the Federal Trade Commission, National Labor Relations Board, and other bodies. In a separate 5-4 vote, the court blocked President Trump's immediate removal of Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, with Chief Justice Roberts writing that removing her would effectively convert for-cause protections to at-will employment.
stories.context: The 1935 Humphrey's Executor ruling had protected independent agency heads from removal for partisan reasons. Justice Sotomayor in dissent warned the majority decision could lead to submission, instability, and even oppression.

stories.headline: European heatwave kills more than 1,300 people as Italy and the Balkans record extreme temperatures
stories.summary: A heatwave that began on June 20 has caused more than 1,300 excess deaths across Europe, according to the World Health Organization, with Italy placing 22 cities under red heat alerts and Croatia, Serbia, Romania, and Hungary reporting temperatures well above seasonal norms. France alone recorded about 1,000 heat-related excess deaths, primarily among elderly people, and funeral homes in Paris were reported to be overwhelmed. An Italian meteorologist said temperatures were expected to remain eight to ten degrees Celsius above average for several more days.
stories.watch_for: Scientists linked the extreme nighttime temperatures to human-caused climate change, which they said made such events 100 times more likely than two decades ago.

stories.headline: Israeli forces shell Syrian village in Deraa province, forcing overnight evacuations
stories.summary: Israeli forces attacked the village of Abdin in Syria's Deraa province near the occupied Golan Heights on June 29, firing shells near civilian homes after residents attempted to block an Israeli army unit's advance, Al Jazeera reported. Families evacuated to neighboring villages overnight and returned the following morning after Israeli forces withdrew, with an unexploded artillery shell documented near a residential home. Syria's Foreign Ministry condemned the attack as a violation of sovereignty, and Turkey's Foreign Ministry called it a breach of international law.
stories.context: Israel expanded its military presence in southern Syria after Bashar al-Assad's government fell in late 2024, moving into UN-monitored buffer zones. The Syrian Sijil Center documented nearly 300 Israeli military operations in Deraa and Quneitra provinces in June alone.

stories.headline: Japanese yen falls to 39-year low against the US dollar as rate-hike expectations drive dollar rally
stories.summary: The Japanese yen fell to its lowest level against the US dollar in 39 years on Monday, Nikkei Asia reported, driven by market expectations that the US Federal Reserve will raise interest rates, which attracts capital to dollar-denominated assets and strengthens the currency. The sharp yen decline heightened speculation that Japanese authorities might intervene in foreign exchange markets to support the currency. A widening interest-rate differential between the United States and Japan was cited as making dollar-denominated assets increasingly attractive to investors.
stories.context: A weaker yen raises import costs for Japan and can contribute to inflationary pressure in an economy that depends heavily on imported raw materials and energy.
